sentences.english: The city was destroyed by the soldiers.
sentences.latin_answer: urbs ā mīlitibus dēlēta est.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Requires perfect passive (dēlēta est) + agent expressed with ā/ab + ablative (ā mīlitibus).

sentences.english: The letters were being carried to Rome.
sentences.latin_answer: epistulae Rōmam ferēbantur.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Requires imperfect passive (ferēbantur) — action ongoing in the past. No agent needed as none is specified.

sentences.english: The slaves had been punished by the master before the guests arrived.
sentences.latin_answer: servī ā dominō pūnītī erant antequam hospitēs advēnērunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Requires pluperfect passive (pūnītī erant) for the earlier action + agent (ā dominō) + temporal clause with antequam + perfect indicative.

sentences.english: With the enemy defeated, the soldiers returned to Rome.
sentences.latin_answer: hostibus victīs, mīlitēs Rōmam rediērunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Requires ablative absolute: noun + perfect passive participle, both in ablative (hostibus victīs). The noun must NOT be the subject of the main clause.

sentences.english: The general, having seen the signal, ordered the attack.
sentences.latin_answer: duce signō vīsō, impetum imperāvit.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Ablative absolute using perfect passive participle (vīsō) agreeing with ablative noun (signō). Note: the general cannot be subject of both clauses — alternative: signō datō with different framing.

sentences.english: While Caesar was speaking, the senators began to whisper.
sentences.latin_answer: Caesare dīcente, senātōrēs mussāre coepērunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Ablative absolute using present active participle (dīcente) agreeing with ablative proper noun (Caesare). Present participle indicates simultaneous action.

sentences.english: The soldiers ran to the gate in order to defend the city.
sentences.latin_answer: mīlitēs ad portam cucurrērunt ut urbem dēfenderent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Requires purpose clause: ut + imperfect subjunctive (dēfenderent) because the main verb is past tense (sequence of tenses).

sentences.english: The mother hid the food so that the children would not go hungry.
sentences.latin_answer: māter cibum cēlāvit nē līberī ēsurirent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Negative purpose clause requires nē (not ut nōn) + imperfect subjunctive (ēsurirent). nē is the standard negative purpose conjunction.

sentences.english: Caesar built a wall around the camp to prevent the enemy from escaping.
sentences.latin_answer: Caesar mūrum circum castra exstruxit nē hostēs effugerent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Negative purpose clause: nē + imperfect subjunctive (effugerent). A common exam pitfall — nē, not ut nōn, for negative purpose.

sentences.english: The general was so brave that all the soldiers admired him.
sentences.latin_answer: dux tam fortis erat ut omnēs mīlitēs eum admīrārentur.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Result clause: tam + adjective in main clause signals result; ut + imperfect subjunctive (admīrārentur) introduces the result clause.

sentences.english: The storm was so fierce that the ships could not leave the harbour.
sentences.latin_answer: tempestās ita saeva erat ut nāvēs portum relinquere nōn possent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Result clause with ita + adjective + ut + imperfect subjunctive (possent). Note nōn is used to negate result clauses (unlike purpose clauses which use nē).

sentences.english: He spoke with such great skill that the judge freed the man.
sentences.latin_answer: tantā arte dīxit ut iūdex virum līberāret.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Result clause introduced by tantā...ut. The degree expression (tantā arte) is in the main clause; ut + imperfect subjunctive (līberāret) gives the result.

sentences.english: The soldiers say that the king has arrived.
sentences.latin_answer: mīlitēs rēgem advēnisse dīcunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Indirect statement after dīcunt: accusative subject (rēgem) + perfect active infinitive (advēnisse). Perfect infinitive because the arrival happened before the speaking.

sentences.english: I know that you are making a mistake.
sentences.latin_answer: sciō tē errāre.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Indirect statement after sciō: accusative subject (tē, accusative of tū) + present active infinitive (errāre). Present infinitive = action simultaneous with main verb.

sentences.english: The general believed that the city had been captured.
sentences.latin_answer: dux crēdēbat urbem captam esse.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Indirect statement after crēdēbat: accusative subject (urbem) + perfect passive infinitive (captam esse). The participle (captam) must agree with the accusative subject in gender and number.

sentences.english: The teacher ordered the students to be quiet.
sentences.latin_answer: magister discipulīs imperāvit ut tacērent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: imperō takes dative + ut + subjunctive (not accusative + infinitive). Dative: discipulīs; ut + imperfect subjunctive: tacērent.

sentences.english: The general urged the soldiers not to flee.
sentences.latin_answer: dux mīlitēs hortātus est nē fugerent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: hortātus est (deponent) + nē + imperfect subjunctive (fugerent) for negative indirect command. Note: hortōr takes accusative, not dative.

sentences.english: Caesar ordered the prisoners to be brought to Rome.
sentences.latin_answer: Caesar iussit captīvōs Rōmam dūcī.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: iubeō takes accusative + infinitive (not ut + subjunctive). captīvōs is accusative; dūcī is present passive infinitive. This is the standard iubeō exception.

sentences.english: After the soldiers had returned, the citizens celebrated.
sentences.latin_answer: postquam mīlitēs rediērunt, cīvēs celebrāvērunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: postquam + perfect indicative (rediērunt) for completed past action. postquam takes the indicative, not the subjunctive.

sentences.english: When the enemy attacked, the guards sounded the alarm.
sentences.latin_answer: ubi hostēs impetum fēcērunt, custōdēs signum dedērunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: ubi + perfect indicative (fēcērunt) for a single completed past event. ubi with indicative = 'when' (factual); with subjunctive it would imply repeated action.

sentences.english: Whenever the teacher entered the room, the students fell silent.
sentences.latin_answer: cum magister in cubiculum intrāret, discipulī silēbant.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: cum + imperfect subjunctive (intrāret) expresses repeated past action ('whenever'). The imperfect in main clause (silēbant) reinforces the habitual aspect.

sentences.english: If you work hard, you will succeed.
sentences.latin_answer: sī dīligenter labōrābis, succēdēs.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Open conditional (future): sī + future indicative in both clauses. In Latin, both the condition and result use future (or future perfect + future) — unlike English which uses present in the 'if' clause.

sentences.english: If the soldiers were brave, they would defeat the enemy.
sentences.latin_answer: sī mīlitēs fortēs essent, hostēs vincerent.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Unfulfilled conditional (present): sī + present subjunctive (essent) + present subjunctive in main clause (vincerent). Implies the soldiers are not in fact brave.

sentences.english: If the general had arrived earlier, the battle would have been won.
sentences.latin_answer: sī dux mātūrius advēnisset, proelium victum esset.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Unfulfilled conditional (past): sī + pluperfect subjunctive (advēnisset) + pluperfect subjunctive in main clause (victum esset). Both conditions must be pluperfect subjunctive for past unfulfilled.

sentences.english: The woman who was standing at the door called out.
sentences.latin_answer: fēmina quae ad iānuam stābat exclāmāvit.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Relative pronoun quae: feminine singular nominative — agrees with antecedent fēmina (feminine singular) in gender and number; nominative because it is the subject of the relative clause.

sentences.english: The soldiers whose weapons had been taken were captured.
sentences.latin_answer: mīlitēs quōrum arma capta erant captī sunt.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Relative pronoun quōrum: masculine plural genitive — agrees with antecedent mīlitēs in gender and number; genitive because it expresses possession ('whose weapons').

sentences.english: I saw the temple which the citizens had built in honour of the goddess.
sentences.latin_answer: templum vīdī quod cīvēs in honōrem deae aedificāverant.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Relative pronoun quod: neuter singular accusative — agrees with antecedent templum (neuter) in gender and number; accusative because it is the object of aedificāverant within the relative clause.

sentences.english: After the letter had been read, the general ordered his soldiers to set out at once.
sentences.latin_answer: epistulā lectā, dux mīlitibus imperāvit ut statim proficīscerentur.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Combines (1) ablative absolute: epistulā lectā (letter having been read) + (2) indirect command: imperō + dative (mīlitibus) + ut + imperfect subjunctive (proficīscerentur).

sentences.english: The soldiers ran so fast that the enemy, terrified, believed that a great army was approaching.
sentences.latin_answer: mīlitēs tam celeriter cucurrērunt ut hostēs perterritī crēderent exercitum magnum appropinquāre.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Combines (1) result clause: tam...ut + imperfect subjunctive (crēderent) + (2) indirect statement after crēderent: accusative (exercitum) + present infinitive (appropinquāre).

sentences.english: The queen, having learned that the enemy had entered the city, fled so that she would not be captured.
sentences.latin_answer: rēgīna, cognōscēns hostēs urbem intrāvisse, fūgit nē caperētur.
sentences.mark_scheme_note: Combines (1) present participle (cognōscēns) with indirect statement (hostēs...intrāvisse: perfect infinitive showing prior action) + (2) negative purpose clause: nē + imperfect subjunctive passive (caperētur).
